from pyspark.sql import functions as F
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

from statsmodels.graphics.tsaplots import plot_acf
from statsmodels.stats.diagnostic import acorr_ljungbox
from arch import arch_model


def run_garch_one_ticker_log_returns_only(
    mbo_spark_df,
    ticker="CL.c.0",
    start_date="2025-11-18",
    end_date="2026-01-20",
    freq="10s",
    single_session_only=None,
    min_points_per_session=30,
    train_frac=0.70,
    val_frac=0.15,
    mean_model="Zero",
    vol_model="GARCH",
    p=1,
    q=1,
    o=0,
    dist="normal",
    scale_returns=100.0,
    realized_vol_window=6,
    trading_days_per_year=252,
    trading_seconds_per_day=23 * 60 * 60,
    show_plots=True
):

    # data extraction
    df = (
        mbo_spark_df
        .filter(
            (F.col("symbol") == ticker) &
            (F.col("session_date") >= F.lit(start_date)) &
            (F.col("session_date") <= F.lit(end_date))
        )
        .select("session_date", "second_bucket", "filled_price")
        .where(F.col("second_bucket").isNotNull())
        .orderBy("session_date", "second_bucket")
    )

    if single_session_only is not None:
        df = df.filter(F.col("session_date") == F.lit(single_session_only))

    pdf = df.toPandas()

    if pdf.empty:
        raise ValueError(f"No data for {ticker} from {start_date} to {end_date}")

    pdf["session_date"] = pd.to_datetime(pdf["session_date"])
    pdf["second_bucket"] = pd.to_datetime(pdf["second_bucket"])
    pdf = pdf.sort_values(["session_date", "second_bucket"]).reset_index(drop=True)

    print(f"Ticker: {ticker}")
    print(f"Raw rows: {len(pdf)}")
    print(f"Sessions: {sorted(pdf['session_date'].dt.strftime('%Y-%m-%d').unique().tolist())}")

    # resample each session
    session_frames = []

    for session, g in pdf.groupby("session_date"):
        g = g.sort_values("second_bucket").copy()
        g = g.dropna(subset=["filled_price"])

        if g.empty:
            continue

        g = g.set_index("second_bucket")
        s = g["filled_price"].resample(freq).last().ffill().dropna()

        if len(s) < min_points_per_session:
            continue

        tmp = s.to_frame(name="filled_price").reset_index()
        tmp["session_date"] = pd.to_datetime(session)
        session_frames.append(tmp)

    if not session_frames:
        raise ValueError("No usable sessions after resampling.")

    ts_df = pd.concat(session_frames, ignore_index=True)
    ts_df = ts_df.sort_values(["session_date", "second_bucket"]).reset_index(drop=True)

    # build log returns
    ts_df["log_price"] = np.log(ts_df["filled_price"])
    ts_df["log_return"] = ts_df.groupby("session_date")["log_price"].diff()
    ts_df = ts_df.dropna(subset=["log_return"]).copy()

    series_return = ts_df.set_index("second_bucket")["log_return"].copy()
    series_session = ts_df.set_index("second_bucket")["session_date"].copy()

    keep_mask = ~series_return.index.duplicated(keep="first")
    series_return = series_return[keep_mask]
    series_session = series_session[keep_mask]

    if len(series_return) < 200:
        raise ValueError("Too few observations for stable GARCH estimation.")

    print(f"Usable return observations: {len(series_return)}")

    # split train, validation, and test
    n = len(series_return)
    train_end = int(n * train_frac)
    val_end = int(n * (train_frac + val_frac))

    train_ret = series_return.iloc[:train_end]
    val_ret = series_return.iloc[train_end:val_end]
    test_ret = series_return.iloc[val_end:]

    print(f"Train: {len(train_ret)} | Val: {len(val_ret)} | Test: {len(test_ret)}")

    if len(train_ret) < 100 or len(val_ret) < 20 or len(test_ret) < 20:
        raise ValueError("One of the splits is too small.")

    # annualization
    freq_seconds = pd.to_timedelta(freq).total_seconds()
    periods_per_day = trading_seconds_per_day / freq_seconds
    annualization_factor = np.sqrt(periods_per_day * trading_days_per_year)

    print(f"Freq: {freq_seconds}s | Periods/day: {periods_per_day} | Ann. factor: {annualization_factor:.2f}")

    # exploratory plots
    if show_plots:
        plt.figure(figsize=(14, 4))
        plt.plot(series_return.index, series_return.values, linewidth=1)
        plt.title(f"{ticker} log returns ({freq})")
        plt.xlabel("Time")
        plt.ylabel("Log return")
        plt.grid(True, alpha=0.3)
        plt.tight_layout()
        plt.show()

        plt.figure(figsize=(14, 4))
        plt.plot(train_ret.index, train_ret.values, linewidth=1)
        plt.title(f"{ticker} train log returns")
        plt.xlabel("Time")
        plt.ylabel("Log return")
        plt.grid(True, alpha=0.3)
        plt.tight_layout()
        plt.show()

        plot_acf(train_ret.dropna(), lags=50)
        plt.title(f"{ticker} ACF of log returns")
        plt.tight_layout()
        plt.show()

        plot_acf(train_ret.dropna() ** 2, lags=50)
        plt.title(f"{ticker} ACF of squared log returns")
        plt.tight_layout()
        plt.show()

    # choose volatility model
    all_scaled = series_return * scale_returns

    vol_key = vol_model.upper().replace("-", "")
    if vol_key == "GARCH":
        arch_kwargs = dict(vol="GARCH", p=p, q=q, o=0)
    elif vol_key == "EGARCH":
        arch_kwargs = dict(vol="EGARCH", p=p, q=q, o=0)
    elif vol_key in {"GJRGARCH", "GJR"}:
        arch_kwargs = dict(vol="GARCH", p=p, o=max(1, o), q=q)
    else:
        raise ValueError("vol_model must be: 'GARCH', 'EGARCH', or 'GJR-GARCH'")

    model = arch_model(
        all_scaled,
        mean=mean_model,
        dist=dist,
        rescale=False,
        **arch_kwargs
    )

    # fit model on train
    results_train = model.fit(last_obs=train_ret.index[-1], disp="off")

    print("\nTrain model summary")
    print(results_train.summary())

    # residual diagnostics
    resid = pd.Series(results_train.resid, index=series_return.index).loc[train_ret.index].dropna()
    cond_vol_train_scaled = pd.Series(
        results_train.conditional_volatility,
        index=series_return.index
    ).loc[train_ret.index].dropna()

    std_resid = (resid / cond_vol_train_scaled).replace([np.inf, -np.inf], np.nan).dropna()

    lb_resid = acorr_ljungbox(std_resid, lags=[10, 20, 30], return_df=True)
    lb_sq = acorr_ljungbox(std_resid ** 2, lags=[10, 20, 30], return_df=True)

    print("\nLjung-Box on standardized residuals:")
    print(lb_resid)

    print("\nLjung-Box on squared standardized residuals:")
    print(lb_sq)

    if show_plots:
        plt.figure(figsize=(14, 4))
        plt.plot(std_resid.index, std_resid.values, linewidth=1)
        plt.title(f"{ticker} standardized residuals (train)")
        plt.grid(True, alpha=0.3)
        plt.tight_layout()
        plt.show()

        plot_acf(std_resid, lags=50)
        plt.title(f"{ticker} ACF of standardized residuals")
        plt.tight_layout()
        plt.show()

        plot_acf(std_resid ** 2, lags=50)
        plt.title(f"{ticker} ACF of squared standardized residuals")
        plt.tight_layout()
        plt.show()

        plt.figure(figsize=(14, 4))
        plt.plot(
            cond_vol_train_scaled.index,
            cond_vol_train_scaled.values / scale_returns,
            linewidth=1
        )
        plt.title(f"{ticker} conditional volatility (train, raw)")
        plt.ylabel("Raw volatility")
        plt.grid(True, alpha=0.3)
        plt.tight_layout()
        plt.show()

        plt.figure(figsize=(14, 4))
        plt.plot(
            cond_vol_train_scaled.index,
            (cond_vol_train_scaled.values / scale_returns) * annualization_factor,
            linewidth=1
        )
        plt.title(f"{ticker} conditional volatility (train, annualized)")
        plt.ylabel("Annualized volatility")
        plt.grid(True, alpha=0.3)
        plt.tight_layout()
        plt.show()

    # validation forecast
    val_forecast_res = results_train.forecast(start=val_ret.index[0], horizon=1, reindex=True)

    val_var_all = val_forecast_res.variance.dropna()
    val_var_scaled = val_var_all.loc[val_ret.index, "h.1"]
    val_vol_forecast = np.sqrt(val_var_scaled) / scale_returns

    val_realized_vol = val_ret.rolling(realized_vol_window).std()
    val_realized_vol_ann = val_realized_vol * annualization_factor
    val_vol_forecast_ann = val_vol_forecast * annualization_factor

    train_vol_const = train_ret.std()
    val_baseline_vol = pd.Series(train_vol_const, index=val_ret.index)
    val_baseline_vol_ann = val_baseline_vol * annualization_factor

    val_eval = pd.concat([
        val_ret.rename("ret"),
        val_realized_vol.rename("realized_vol"),
        val_realized_vol_ann.rename("realized_vol_ann"),
        val_vol_forecast.rename("forecast_vol"),
        val_vol_forecast_ann.rename("forecast_vol_ann"),
        val_baseline_vol.rename("baseline_vol"),
        val_baseline_vol_ann.rename("baseline_vol_ann"),
    ], axis=1).dropna()

    if val_eval.empty:
        raise ValueError("Validation  is empty ")

    eps = 1e-12

    # validation metrics
    val_mae_vol = np.mean(np.abs(val_eval["realized_vol"] - val_eval["forecast_vol"]))
    val_rmse_vol = np.sqrt(np.mean((val_eval["realized_vol"] - val_eval["forecast_vol"]) ** 2))
    val_mse_vol = np.mean((val_eval["realized_vol"] - val_eval["forecast_vol"]) ** 2)

    val_baseline_mae_vol = np.mean(np.abs(val_eval["realized_vol"] - val_eval["baseline_vol"]))
    val_baseline_rmse_vol = np.sqrt(np.mean((val_eval["realized_vol"] - val_eval["baseline_vol"]) ** 2))
    val_baseline_mse_vol = np.mean((val_eval["realized_vol"] - val_eval["baseline_vol"]) ** 2)

    val_realized_var = np.maximum(val_eval["realized_vol"].values ** 2, eps)
    val_forecast_var = np.maximum(val_eval["forecast_vol"].values ** 2, eps)
    val_baseline_var = np.maximum(val_eval["baseline_vol"].values ** 2, eps)
    val_qlike = np.mean(np.log(val_forecast_var) + val_realized_var / val_forecast_var)
    val_baseline_qlike = np.mean(np.log(val_baseline_var) + val_realized_var / val_baseline_var)

    val_mae_vol_ann = np.mean(np.abs(val_eval["realized_vol_ann"] - val_eval["forecast_vol_ann"]))
    val_rmse_vol_ann = np.sqrt(np.mean((val_eval["realized_vol_ann"] - val_eval["forecast_vol_ann"]) ** 2))
    val_mse_vol_ann = np.mean((val_eval["realized_vol_ann"] - val_eval["forecast_vol_ann"]) ** 2)

    val_baseline_mae_vol_ann = np.mean(np.abs(val_eval["realized_vol_ann"] - val_eval["baseline_vol_ann"]))
    val_baseline_rmse_vol_ann = np.sqrt(np.mean((val_eval["realized_vol_ann"] - val_eval["baseline_vol_ann"]) ** 2))
    val_baseline_mse_vol_ann = np.mean((val_eval["realized_vol_ann"] - val_eval["baseline_vol_ann"]) ** 2)

    val_realized_var_ann = np.maximum(val_eval["realized_vol_ann"].values ** 2, eps)
    val_forecast_var_ann = np.maximum(val_eval["forecast_vol_ann"].values ** 2, eps)
    val_baseline_var_ann = np.maximum(val_eval["baseline_vol_ann"].values ** 2, eps)
    val_qlike_ann = np.mean(np.log(val_forecast_var_ann) + val_realized_var_ann / val_forecast_var_ann)
    val_baseline_qlike_ann = np.mean(np.log(val_baseline_var_ann) + val_realized_var_ann / val_baseline_var_ann)

    print("\nVALIDATION METRICS (raw)")
    print(f"  GARCH    — MAE: {val_mae_vol:.10f}  RMSE: {val_rmse_vol:.10f}  QLIKE: {val_qlike:.10f}")
    print(f"  Baseline — MAE: {val_baseline_mae_vol:.10f}  RMSE: {val_baseline_rmse_vol:.10f}  QLIKE: {val_baseline_qlike:.10f}")
    print("VALIDATION METRICS (annualized)")
    print(f"  GARCH    — MAE: {val_mae_vol_ann:.10f}  RMSE: {val_rmse_vol_ann:.10f}  QLIKE: {val_qlike_ann:.10f}")
    print(f"  Baseline — MAE: {val_baseline_mae_vol_ann:.10f}  RMSE: {val_baseline_rmse_vol_ann:.10f}  QLIKE: {val_baseline_qlike_ann:.10f}")

    # validation plots
    if show_plots:
        plt.figure(figsize=(14, 5))
        plt.plot(train_ret.index, train_ret.rolling(realized_vol_window).std(), label="Train realized vol", linewidth=1)
        plt.plot(val_eval.index, val_eval["realized_vol"], label="Val realized vol", linewidth=1)
        plt.plot(val_eval.index, val_eval["forecast_vol"], label="Val GARCH forecast", linewidth=1)
        plt.plot(val_eval.index, val_eval["baseline_vol"], label="Val baseline (const)", linewidth=1, linestyle="--")
        plt.title(f"{ticker} validation volatility forecast (raw)")
        plt.ylabel("Raw volatility")
        plt.legend()
        plt.grid(True, alpha=0.3)
        plt.tight_layout()
        plt.show()

        plt.figure(figsize=(14, 5))
        plt.plot(val_eval.index, val_eval["realized_vol_ann"], label="Val realized vol (ann)", linewidth=1)
        plt.plot(val_eval.index, val_eval["forecast_vol_ann"], label="Val GARCH forecast (ann)", linewidth=1)
        plt.plot(val_eval.index, val_eval["baseline_vol_ann"], label="Val baseline (ann)", linewidth=1, linestyle="--")
        plt.title(f"{ticker} validation volatility forecast (annualized)")
        plt.ylabel("Annualized volatility")
        plt.legend()
        plt.grid(True, alpha=0.3)
        plt.tight_layout()
        plt.show()

    # fit model on train and validation
    results_train_val = model.fit(last_obs=val_ret.index[-1], disp="off")

    print("\nTrain+validation summary")
    print(results_train_val.summary())

    # test forecast
    test_forecast_res = results_train_val.forecast(start=test_ret.index[0], horizon=1, reindex=True)

    test_var_all = test_forecast_res.variance.dropna()
    test_var_scaled = test_var_all.loc[test_ret.index, "h.1"]

    train_val_ret = series_return.iloc[:val_end]

    test_baseline_vol = pd.Series(train_val_vol_const, index=test_ret.index)

    test_eval = pd.concat([

    # test metrics

    test_mse_vol_ann = np.mean((test_eval["realized_vol_ann"] - test_eval["forecast_vol_ann"]) ** 2)

    test_baseline_mae_vol_ann = np.mean(np.abs(test_eval["realized_vol_ann"] - test_eval["baseline_vol_ann"]))
    test_baseline_rmse_vol_ann = np.sqrt(np.mean((test_eval["realized_vol_ann"] - test_eval["baseline_vol_ann"]) ** 2))

# multi ticker wrapper
def run_garch_multiple_tickers(mbo_spark_df, tickers, **garch_kwargs):
    results = {}
    skipped = {}

    for ticker in tickers:
        print(f"\nChecking ticker: {ticker}")
        try:
            results[ticker] = run_garch_one_ticker_log_returns_only(
                mbo_spark_df=mbo_spark_df,
                ticker=ticker,
                **garch_kwargs
            )
        except Exception as e:
            print(f"Could not run {ticker}: {e}")
            skipped[ticker] = str(e)

    return {
        "results": results,
        "skipped": skipped
    }

records.content:
Trains Ridge and Lasso regression models on the 1-second MBO feature table to predict rv_next_60 and rv_next_300.

Local: reads Parquet from ~/Downloads/mbo_features_table, local Spark session

Colab / EMR: reads from S3, connects via Spark Connect on port 15002

Run EDA_basic.ipynb and EDA_advanced.ipynb first.

EDA key findings motivating this notebook:

rv_past_60 and ret_std_60 were the strongest predictors of rv_next_60 (|corr| ≈ 0.655 each)

Event-count features (n_cancel_60, n_add_60, n_trade_60) showed moderate correlation (~0.29–0.30), suggesting order book activity adds signal beyond recent volatility alone

Imbalance features (order_imbalance_60, size_imbalance_60) had near-zero linear correlation, hinting at nonlinear relationships worth exploring in later models

Reference:

Bucci, A. (2021). Realized Volatility Forecasting with Neural Networks. Journal of Financial Econometrics. 

Zhang et al. (2021)

Patton, A.J. (2011). Volatility forecast comparison using imperfect
volatility proxies. Journal of Econometrics, 160(1), 246–256.


Patton (2011)

https://​stats​.stackexchange​.com​/questions​/225877​/interpreting​-qlike​-and​-mse​-loss​-function​-patton​-2011

Exclude anomalous sessions (holidays + known bad final session 2026-01-16)

Filter to active seconds (rv_past_60 > 0) , quiet seconds are structurally zero and pull the model toward zero predictions

Add time-of-day features , strong intraday U-shape in volatility

# anomalous / holiday session dates flagged in EDA_advanced
EXCLUDE_DATES = [
    '2025-11-28',  # Thanksgiving (US)
    '2025-12-24',  # Christmas Eve
    '2025-12-25',  # Christmas
    '2025-12-31',  # New Year's Eve
    '2026-01-01',  # New Year's Day
    '2026-01-16',  # final session (abnormal activity)
]

    # ran without rv_next_60 , and then added rv_next_60 filter,When you filter only rv_past_60 > 0 you keep seconds where the past was active but the next 60 seconds could still be completely quiet (rv_next_60 = 0).
df = df_raw \
    .filter(~F.col('session_date').cast('string').isin(EXCLUDE_DATES)) \
    .filter(F.col('rv_past_60') > 0) \
    .filter(F.col('rv_next_60') > 0) \
    .filter(F.col('rv_next_60').isNotNull()) \
    .filter(F.col('rv_next_300').isNotNull())

# time-of-day features
df = df \
    .withColumn('hour_utc',         F.hour('second_bucket').cast(DoubleType())) \
    .withColumn('minute_of_hour',   F.minute('second_bucket').cast(DoubleType()))

# Pre-encode symbol as numeric , avoids StringIndexer over Spark Connect

symbol_idx_expr = F.when(F.lit(False), F.lit(None).cast(DoubleType()))
for i, sym in enumerate(SYMBOLS_SORTED):
    symbol_idx_expr = symbol_idx_expr.when(F.col('symbol') == sym, float(i))
symbol_idx_expr = symbol_idx_expr.otherwise(float(len(SYMBOLS_SORTED)))

active_count = df.count()

records.content:
StringIndexer encodes the symbol categorical column as a numeric index

VectorAssembler collects all feature columns into a single features vector

StandardScaler normalises to zero mean / unit variance, essential because n_events_60 (0–11k) and rv_past_60 (0–0.01) are on completely different scales

LinearRegression with regParam (λ) and elasticNetParam (0=Ridge, 1=Lasso)

FEATURE_COLS = [
    'rv_past_60',
    'n_events_60', 'n_add_60', 'n_cancel_60', 'n_modify_60',
    'n_trade_60',  'n_fill_60', 'sum_size_60',
    'order_imbalance_60', 'size_imbalance_60',
    'ret_std_60',
    'hour_utc', 'minute_of_hour',
    'symbol_idx',
]
TARGET_COLS  = ['rv_next_60', 'rv_next_300']
COLLECT_COLS = FEATURE_COLS + TARGET_COLS + ['symbol', 'session_date']

# cast in Spark (runs on EMR)
for c in FEATURE_COLS:
    train_df = train_df.withColumn(c, F.col(c).cast(DoubleType()))
    val_df   = val_df.withColumn(c,   F.col(c).cast(DoubleType()))
    test_df  = test_df.withColumn(c,  F.col(c).cast(DoubleType()))

# collect to pandas  EMR handles S3 read, driver gets feature rows
print('Collecting splits to pandas (this may take a minute)...')
train_pd = train_df.select(COLLECT_COLS).toPandas()
print(f'  train : {len(train_pd):,} rows')
val_pd   = val_df.select(COLLECT_COLS).toPandas()
print(f'  val   : {len(val_pd):,} rows')
test_pd  = test_df.select(COLLECT_COLS).toPandas()
print(f'  test  : {len(test_pd):,} rows')

# z-score scale using train stats only (no leakage)
feat_means = train_pd[FEATURE_COLS].mean()
feat_stds  = train_pd[FEATURE_COLS].std().replace(0, 1)

def scale(df):
    X = (df[FEATURE_COLS] - feat_means) / feat_stds
    return X.values

X_train = scale(train_pd)
X_val   = scale(val_pd)
X_test  = scale(test_pd)

print(f'Feature matrix shapes — train:{X_train.shape}  val:{X_val.shape}  test:{X_test.shape}')




from sklearn.linear_model import Ridge, Lasso

def build_model(reg_type: str, alpha: float):
    """Return a fitted-ready sklearn Ridge or Lasso model."""
    if reg_type == 'Ridge':
        return Ridge(alpha=alpha, fit_intercept=True, max_iter=1000)
    else:
        return Lasso(alpha=alpha, fit_intercept=True, max_iter=1000)

records.content:
# Floor predictions at 1st percentile of training RV avoids QLIKE explosion
# from clipping to 0 (which makes y/eps astronomically large)
RV_FLOOR = float(train_pd['rv_next_60'].quantile(0.01))
print(f'RV floor (1st pct): {RV_FLOOR:.8f}')

def evaluate(model, X, y, label, floor=None):
    yhat = model.predict(X)
    yhat = np.maximum(yhat, floor if floor is not None else RV_FLOOR)
    rmse  = float(np.sqrt(np.mean((y - yhat) ** 2)))
    ss_res = np.sum((y - yhat) ** 2)
    ss_tot = np.sum((y - y.mean()) ** 2)
    r2    = float(1 - ss_res / ss_tot) if ss_tot > 0 else float('nan')
    eps   = 1e-10
    qlike = float(np.mean(np.log(yhat + eps) + y / (yhat + eps)))
    print(f'  {label:5s}  RMSE={rmse:.6f}  R²={r2:.4f}  QLIKE={qlike:.4f}')
    return {'rmse': rmse, 'r2': r2, 'qlike': qlike}

TARGET      = 'rv_next_60'

ridge_results = []

print(f'Ridge sweep — target: {TARGET}')
print('-' * 60)

for lam in RIDGE_PARAMS:
    with mlflow.start_run(run_name=f'Ridge_lam{lam}_{TARGET}'):
        mlflow.set_tag('author', 'Nandhu Sriram')
        mlflow.set_tag('model_type', 'Ridge')
        mlflow.set_tag("execution_env", "colab" if IN_COLAB else "pycharm-local")

        model.fit(X_train, y_train)

        m_val  = evaluate(model, X_val,  y_val,  'val')

records.content:
RV is right-skewed (heavy upper tail). Fitting on log1p(rv) pulls the distribution closer to Gaussian, which helps OLS. Predictions are back-transformed with expm1 before evaluation so metrics are on the original scale.

# ── rv_next_60 log1p ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────
TARGET_LOG   = 'rv_next_60'
y_train_log  = np.log1p(train_pd[TARGET_LOG].values)
y_val_log    = val_pd[TARGET_LOG].values    # evaluate on original scale
y_test_log   = test_pd[TARGET_LOG].values

best_lam     = best_ridge[0]
model_log    = build_model('Ridge', alpha=best_lam)
model_log.fit(X_train, y_train_log)

def eval_log1p(model, X, y_raw, label):
    yhat = np.expm1(model.predict(X))
    yhat = np.maximum(yhat, RV_FLOOR)
    rmse  = float(np.sqrt(np.mean((y_raw - yhat) ** 2)))
    ss_res = np.sum((y_raw - yhat) ** 2)
    ss_tot = np.sum((y_raw - y_raw.mean()) ** 2)
    r2    = float(1 - ss_res / ss_tot) if ss_tot > 0 else float('nan')
    eps   = 1e-10
    qlike = float(np.mean(np.log(yhat + eps) + y_raw / (yhat + eps)))
    print(f'  {label:5s}  RMSE={rmse:.6f}  R²={r2:.4f}  QLIKE={qlike:.4f}')
    return {'rmse': rmse, 'r2': r2, 'qlike': qlike}

print('--- log1p results (rv_next_60, back-transformed) ---')
m_val_log  = eval_log1p(model_log, X_val,  y_val_log,  'val')
m_test_log = eval_log1p(model_log, X_test, y_test_log, 'test')

mlflow.set_experiment(EXPERIMENT_NAME)
with mlflow.start_run(run_name=f'Ridge_log1p_lam{best_lam}_rv_next_60'):
    mlflow.set_tag('author', 'Nandhu Sriram')
    mlflow.set_tag('model_type', 'Ridge')
    mlflow.set_tag('target_transform', 'log1p')
    mlflow.set_tag("execution_env", "colab" if IN_COLAB else "pycharm-local")
    mlflow.log_params({'model_type': 'Ridge', 'target_transform': 'log1p', 'reg_param': best_lam, 'target': 'rv_next_60'})
    mlflow.log_metrics({'val_r2': m_val_log['r2'], 'val_rmse': m_val_log['rmse'], 'val_qlike': m_val_log['qlike'],
                        'test_r2': m_test_log['r2'], 'test_rmse': m_test_log['rmse'], 'test_qlike': m_test_log['qlike']})

# ── rv_next_300 log1p ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────
y_train_300_log = np.log1p(train_pd[TARGET_300].values)
best_lam_300    = best_ridge_300[0]
model_log_300   = build_model('Ridge', alpha=best_lam_300)
model_log_300.fit(X_train, y_train_300_log)

print('--- log1p results (rv_next_300, back-transformed) ---')
m_val_log_300  = eval_log1p(model_log_300, X_val,  y_val_300,  'val')
m_test_log_300 = eval_log1p(model_log_300, X_test, y_test_300, 'test')

with mlflow.start_run(run_name=f'Ridge_log1p_lam{best_lam_300}_rv_next_300'):
    mlflow.log_params({'model_type': 'Ridge', 'target_transform': 'log1p', 'reg_param': best_lam_300, 'target': 'rv_next_300'})
    mlflow.log_metrics({'val_r2': m_val_log_300['r2'], 'val_rmse': m_val_log_300['rmse'],
                        'test_r2': m_test_log_300['r2'], 'test_rmse': m_test_log_300['rmse']})

# ── comparison tables ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────
def _m(model, X, y):
    yhat = np.maximum(model.predict(X), RV_FLOOR)
    rmse = float(np.sqrt(np.mean((y-yhat)**2)))
    r2   = float(1 - np.sum((y-yhat)**2)/np.sum((y-y.mean())**2))
    eps  = 1e-10
    return {'RMSE': f'{rmse:.6f}', 'R²': f'{r2:.4f}',
            'QLIKE': f'{float(np.mean(np.log(yhat+eps)+y/(yhat+eps))):.4f}'}

print('\n=== Test-set comparison — rv_next_60 ===')
cmp60 = pd.DataFrame([
    {'Model': f'Ridge (λ={best_ridge[0]})',      'Transform': 'none',  **_m(best_ridge_model, X_test, y_test)},
    {'Model': f'Lasso (λ={best_lasso[0]})',      'Transform': 'none',  **_m(best_lasso_model, X_test, y_test)},
    {'Model': f'Ridge log1p (λ={best_lam})',     'Transform': 'log1p', **{k: f'{v:.6f}' if k=="RMSE" else f'{v:.4f}' for k,v in
        dict(zip(['RMSE','R²','QLIKE'],[m_test_log['rmse'],m_test_log['r2'],m_test_log['qlike']])).items()}},
]).set_index('Model')
print(cmp60.to_string())

print('\n=== Test-set comparison — rv_next_300 ===')
cmp300 = pd.DataFrame([
    {'Model': f'Ridge (λ={best_ridge_300[0]})',     'Transform': 'none',  **_m(best_ridge_300[4], X_test, y_test_300)},
    {'Model': f'Ridge log1p (λ={best_lam_300})',    'Transform': 'log1p', **{k: f'{v:.6f}' if k=="RMSE" else f'{v:.4f}' for k,v in
        dict(zip(['RMSE','R²','QLIKE'],[m_test_log_300['rmse'],m_test_log_300['r2'],m_test_log_300['qlike']])).items()}},
]).set_index('Model')
print(cmp300.to_string())

spark.stop()
print('Spark session stopped.')

records.content:
Across both Ridge and Lasso regression, the results point in the same direction.

rv_past_60 was the dominant predictor, but the broader MBO microstructure feature set improved Ridge performance meaningfully.

At the shorter 60-second horizon, Ridge achieved solid performance.

Ridge (λ=1.0): RMSE=0.000218, R²=0.533, QLIKE=-7.037

Lasso (λ=0.0001): RMSE=0.000269, R²=0.292, QLIKE=-6.977

Ridge substantially outperformed Lasso, suggesting that retaining all 13 features even with small coefficients adds predictive value.

At the longer 300-second horizon, Ridge held up well.

R² slightly improved over the 60-second target, indicating that the feature set captures medium-term volatility structure effectively.

Lasso’s aggressive sparsity tells a clear story:

At λ=0.0001, Lasso zeroed out all features except rv_past_60, effectively reducing to a univariate model.

The resulting R²=0.29 vs Ridge’s R²=0.53 confirms the other 12 features carry real signal that Lasso discards.

The log1p target transform yielded no improvement (identical metrics), indicating the residuals are not heavily right-skewed enough to benefit from a Gaussian pull.

Overall takeaway:

recent realized volatility (rv_past_60) is the strongest single predictor, but microstructure features add meaningful lift when kept via Ridge regularization

linear models explain ~53–55% of variance at both horizons — a strong baseline before nonlinear models (XGBoost, LSTM)

the relationship between microstructure features and volatility is likely nonlinear, as Lasso’s feature elimination and the remaining unexplained variance both suggest

Linear regression serves as a strong interpretable baseline for realized volatility forecasting. Ridge regression (λ=1.0) explained ~53% of variance at the 60-second horizon and ~55% at the 300-second horizon with the slightly better performance at 5 minutes confirming that microstructure features capture trends that persist beyond a single second. Lasso’s collapse to a single feature (rv_past_60) highlights that the feature relationships are likely nonlinear, and that regularization alone cannot recover them in a linear framework. The consistent QLIKE improvement of Ridge over Lasso further supports this.

The results set a clear benchmark: any nonlinear model (XGBoost, LSTM) would likely outperform linear regression.

Traditional time-series models like ARIMA or GARCH would likely outperform plain linear regression too. However, neither incorporates order book microstructure features, which our results show carry meaningful predictive signal beyond recent volatility alone.

The split between metrics is meaningful: Lasso achieves lower QLIKE while Ridge achieves lower RMSE. RMSE treats over- and under-prediction equally, so Ridge wins by fitting the full range of volatility values. QLIKE heavily penalizes under-predicting volatility — missing a spike is far more costly than over-hedging. Lasso’s sparse model leans on rv_past_60 (the persistence signal), making it less likely to predict dangerously low vol. In plain terms: Ridge is more accurate on average; Lasso is safer when it matters most (risk management, margin calculations).

records.content:
RMSE penalizes over-prediction and under-prediction equally.
In volatility forecasting, underestimating risk is more dangerous
than overestimating it,a trader who predicts low volatility and
takes full exposure into a volatile market faces unexpected large losses.

QLIKE addresses this by penalizing underestimates more heavily.

Formula
QLIKE = mean( log(predicted vol) + actual vol / predicted vol )

When predicted vol is too low, the second term blows up.
When predicted vol is too high, the first term grows slowly.
Result: underestimation is punished more than overestimation.

Lower QLIKE is better.

Our Ridge model achieved a lower QLIKE than both Lasso and the
persistence baseline — meaning it was better at avoiding the
dangerous class of underestimates.

Citation: Patton, A.J. (2011). Volatility forecast comparison
using imperfect volatility proxies. Journal of Econometrics,
160(1), 246–256. 

Patton (2011)

# ── QLIKE visualization ───────────────────────────────────────────────────
# Panel 1: MSE vs QLIKE penalty curves , shows the asymmetry
# Panel 2: Model ranking comparison , RMSE vs QLIKE side by side


import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np

eps = 1e-10

# ── helper functions ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────
def rmse_fn(y_true, y_pred):
    return float(np.sqrt(np.mean((y_true - y_pred) ** 2)))

def qlike_fn(y_true, y_pred):
    yp = np.maximum(y_pred, eps)
    return float(np.mean(np.log(yp) + y_true / yp))

# ── panel 1: penalty curves ───────────────────────────────────────────────
y_true_fixed = 1.0
y_pred_range = np.linspace(0.1, 3.0, 300)

mse_vals   = (y_true_fixed - y_pred_range) ** 2
qlike_vals = np.log(y_pred_range + eps) + y_true_fixed / (y_pred_range + eps)

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(13, 4))

# MSE
axes[0].plot(y_pred_range, mse_vals, color="coral", linewidth=2)
axes[0].axvline(x=1.0, color="gray", linestyle="--",
                alpha=0.6, label="Perfect forecast")
axes[0].fill_between(
    y_pred_range[y_pred_range < 1.0],
    mse_vals[y_pred_range < 1.0],
    alpha=0.15, color="red", label="Underestimate region"
)
axes[0].fill_between(
    y_pred_range[y_pred_range > 1.0],
    mse_vals[y_pred_range > 1.0],
    alpha=0.15, color="blue", label="Overestimate region"
)
axes[0].set_title("MSE — symmetric penalty", fontsize=12)
axes[0].set_xlabel("Predicted volatility (σ̂)")
axes[0].set_ylabel("Loss")
axes[0].legend(fontsize=9)
axes[0].set_ylim(0, 4)

# QLIKE
axes[1].plot(y_pred_range, qlike_vals, color="steelblue", linewidth=2)
axes[1].axvline(x=1.0, color="gray", linestyle="--",
                alpha=0.6, label="Perfect forecast")
axes[1].fill_between(
    y_pred_range[y_pred_range < 1.0],
    qlike_vals[y_pred_range < 1.0],
    alpha=0.15, color="red", label="Underestimate region"
)
axes[1].fill_between(
    y_pred_range[y_pred_range > 1.0],
    qlike_vals[y_pred_range > 1.0],
    alpha=0.15, color="blue", label="Overestimate region"
)
axes[1].set_title("QLIKE — underestimation costs more", fontsize=12)
axes[1].set_xlabel("Predicted volatility (σ̂)")
axes[1].set_ylabel("Loss")
axes[1].legend(fontsize=9)
axes[1].set_ylim(-2, 6)

plt.suptitle(
    "MSE vs QLIKE penalty curves (actual variance = 1.0)\n"
    "QLIKE rises more steeply left of the perfect forecast line"
    " — underestimation is penalized more heavily",
    y=1.04, fontsize=11
)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

# ── panel 2: model ranking ────────────────────────────────────────────────
# get predictions using your existing fitted models
ridge_pred   = np.maximum(best_ridge_model.predict(X_test), RV_FLOOR)
lasso_pred   = np.maximum(best_lasso_model.predict(X_test), RV_FLOOR)
log_pred     = np.maximum(np.expm1(model_log.predict(X_test)), RV_FLOOR)
persist_pred = train_pd["rv_past_60"].mean() * np.ones_like(y_test)
persist_pred = np.maximum(persist_pred, RV_FLOOR)

model_names = [
    "Persistence",
    f"Ridge λ={best_ridge[0]}",
    f"Lasso λ={best_lasso[0]}",
    "Ridge log1p"
]
predictions = [persist_pred, ridge_pred, lasso_pred, log_pred]

rmse_scores  = [rmse_fn(y_test, p)  for p in predictions]
qlike_scores = [qlike_fn(y_test, p) for p in predictions]

# normalize for visual comparison
rmse_norm  = np.array(rmse_scores) / max(rmse_scores)
qlike_norm = np.array(qlike_scores) - min(qlike_scores)

colors = ["#d0d0d0", "#2c7bb6", "#74add1", "#1a5276"]
x      = np.arange(len(model_names))

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(13, 5))

axes[0].bar(x, rmse_norm, color=colors, edgecolor="white", width=0.6)
axes[0].set_xticks(x)
axes[0].set_xticklabels(model_names, rotation=15, ha="right", fontsize=9)
axes[0].set_title("RMSE ranking\n(lower = better)", fontsize=11)
axes[0].set_ylabel("Normalized RMSE")

axes[1].bar(x, qlike_norm, color=colors, edgecolor="white", width=0.6)
axes[1].set_xticks(x)
axes[1].set_xticklabels(model_names, rotation=15, ha="right", fontsize=9)
axes[1].set_title("QLIKE ranking\n(lower = better)", fontsize=11)
axes[1].set_ylabel("QLIKE (shifted to 0 baseline)")

plt.suptitle(
    "Do RMSE and QLIKE agree on model ranking?\n"
    "If they disagree, QLIKE is capturing something RMSE misses",
    y=1.02, fontsize=11
)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

# ── print summary table ───────────────────────────────────────────────────
print("\n=== RMSE vs QLIKE comparison — rv_next_60 test set ===")
print(f"{'Model':<28} {'RMSE':>12} {'QLIKE':>10}")
print("-" * 54)
for name, r, q in zip(model_names, rmse_scores, qlike_scores):
    print(f"{name:<28} {r:>12.6f} {q:>10.4f}")
print()
print("Lower RMSE  = smaller average prediction error")
print("Lower QLIKE = better at not underestimating volatility")
print()
